author: @rodm4151
text: Good info sir, a lot of industrial electricians have difficulty understanding the relationship between in rush curents ( locked rotor current ) and the normal running currents between unloaded and fully loaded or "FLA". Understanding how the back EMF produced drops the high inrush current after the motor reaches it's rated speed to counteract the input EMF otherwise if the motor rotor did not move the in rush current would remain high and trip your motor overload protection.

author: @Buzzhumma
text: Yeah I recently made a pulse motor with 1mm thick magnets and they weren't strong enough to generate any energy . The 2mm magnets i replaced them with were  though . 1 mm thicker yet 4-5 times greater strength . So so essentially the weren't strong enough and I don't have enough windings nor speed to overcome the resistance of even the lightest load . I pretty much experienced exactly what Rob is explaining in this video . 😂

author: @windzor
text: I think some filaments in bulbs will increase in resistance as they get hot and the current will then decrease some.  When the bulb is cold, the inrush if high current could cause the filament to melt.
